answer: Argon2id with parameters 64MB memory, 3 iterations, and parallelism 4.
Refresh tokens expire in 7 days and are rotated on every use.
PostgreSQL 15.4, upgraded from 14.9.
API Gateway throttles at 5000 burst and 2000 steady.
Argon2id was chosen for better side-channel resistance.
Drizzle was chosen for zero runtime dependencies and SQL-like TypeScript ergonomics.
They validate RS256 JWTs using JWKS (/.well-known/jwks.json); gateway Lambda authorizer also validates via JWKS and injects claims.
Rate limiting is done at ALB to absorb DDoS traffic before requests reach containers/app code.
Gateway schema validation -> Lambda authorizer JWT validation via JWKS and claims injection -> VPC Link -> ALB per-IP rate limit -> Portfolio service RBAC/role hierarchy check before rebalance.

If JWKS is down, downstream token validation fails across gateway authorizer and services that verify JWTs; token issuance continues because the private key is local. There is an LRU JWKS cache (1000 entries, 5-minute TTL grace period).
Real-time portfolio alerting is planned for Q3 and depends on BOTH notify-service and gateway WebSocket API.
Yes. It was fixed by using Unix timestamps instead of Date objects for expiry handling.

Implement by emitting auth events to Notification Service and delivering through the existing webhook system: registration endpoint POST /api/v1/notifications/webhooks/register with exponential backoff retries (5 attempts over 24h).
100 maximum (pool size 20 per instance x 5 Auth instances).
Pool size (20/instance) caps concurrent DB connections but does not resolve Celery memory growth during large portfolio/tax-lot recalcs; the memory issue is still under investigation.
No. Fargate Spot is only for batch workloads (portfolio-recalc, digest), not always-on services like Auth due to interruption risk.
The stateless JWT/JWKS architecture doctrine (multi-service verification without shared secrets) argues against per-instance in-memory session caches.
30-day backups provide historical restore points, while DR targets (RPO 1h, RTO 4h with us-west-2 replication) define failover/recovery timeliness; they are complementary controls.
Connection settings had to use sslmode=verify-full with global-bundle.pem after the RDS upgrade.
id, account_id, security_id, acquisition_date, cost_basis, quantity, adjusted, created_at.
After password verification, login requires MFA when enabled (TOTP step) before issuing tokens.
10 recovery codes are generated; each is single-use and permanently consumed when used.
Exponential backoff with 5 retry attempts over 24 hours.

At rest: passwords hashed with Argon2id (64MB/3/4), PII encrypted with AES-256-GCM using per-tenant keys. In transit: mTLS via Envoy/App Mesh, DB connections require sslmode=verify-full with global-bundle.pem. Token security uses RS256 with JWKS validation, and secrets rotate every 90 days.

Client request enters API Gateway for schema validation, passes through VPC Link to ALB rate limits, Auth handlePostLogin verifies password via Argon2id, MFA challenge is required if enabled (TOTP or recovery code), then Auth issues RS256 access token (15 min) and refresh token (7 day, rotate-on-use). Downstream requests are authorized via Lambda/JWKS claims validation.

Apply existing cross-service patterns: RS256 JWT trust via JWKS validation, gateway + VPC Link + ALB protection, ECS/Fargate deployment model, mTLS through Envoy/App Mesh, RFC7807 error format with traceId, connection pool limits (20/instance), security controls (AES-256-GCM for PII, 90-day secret rotation), and no Spot for always-on paths.

At 1500 req/min from one IP, API Gateway global throttle is not the first bottleneck here, but ALB per-IP limit (1000/min/IP) drops excess traffic before Auth containers. Roughly 1000/min can reach Auth; ~500/min is rate-limited upstream.

JWT signing key dependency map: Auth Service private key for token issuance; JWKS endpoint publishes public keys; Gateway Lambda authorizer depends on JWKS for verification and claims injection; all JWT-verifying services (Auth, Portfolio, Notification, and any new service) depend on JWKS/public key availability.

Commit/PR flow includes Claude Code review before merge, then GitHub Actions runs lint -> typecheck -> test -> build -> push image to ECR -> deploy to ECS. Production rollout uses blue-green ALB target switching for zero-downtime. Fargate Spot is used only for batch jobs.
